Technological Forecasting & Social Change 181 (2022) 121740 Available online 17 May 2022 0040-1625/© 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by- A preliminary assessment of the performance of DeFi cryptocurrencies in relation to other financial assets, volatility, and user-generated content Juan Pi˜neiro-Chousa a,*, M. ´Angeles L´opez-Cabarcos b, Aleksandar Sevic c, Isaac Gonz´alez- a Accounting and Finance at Santiago de Compostela University, Spain b Business Administration at Santiago de Compostela University, Spain c Accounting and Finance at Trinity College of Dublin, Ireland d Santiago de Compostela University, Spain A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T After the so-called “crypto-winter”, decentralised finance (DeFi) is reviving interest in cryptocurrency amongst the scientific community, public and private institutions, and investors. DeFi is a novel disruptive process that promotes the use of blockchain technology for creating and issuing all kinds of financial products and services. This study aimed to measure the relationship amongst the returns of DeFi tokens, other traditional assets, and user-generated content. While the relationship between other crypto assets and traditional assets has been researched, this has not been done on DeFi assets. This study uses a logit-probit model over a database comprising the daily returns of 13 DeFi, VIX, S&P GSCI Crude Oil Index, and S&P GSCI Gold Index, and the daily variation in DeFi mentions in Telegram chats and Twitter. The results show that all variables except the S&P GSCI crude oil index returns and the daily variation in Twitter mentions were significant. This suggests that DeFi acts, similar to other crypto assets, as a safe haven. This study contributes to the literature on decentralised finance tokens as investment assets, which requires much more research. The financial crisis of 2007 had an unexpected effect; an unknown hacker published a paper (Nakamoto, 2008) describing an unsupervised digital currency with the aim of avoiding the need for a central au­ thority. After this paper (Nakamoto, 2008), Satoshi released an open-source software that started Bitcoin as a decentralised digital cryptocurrency. The lack of central governance is a key feature that attracts interest and at the same time raises concerns about its risks (Grant and Hogan, 2015). The cost of avoiding a central node increased when Bitcoin began to increase in popularity because of electricity consumption (Fairley, 2017). The increase in transaction fees to keep mining profitable makes it unusable as a payment system for small amounts of money. Satoshi’s paper talks about “electronic cash” to make “electronic payments”, but in reality after years of being in use, Bitcoin is mainly used as an asset (Baur et al., 2018), either comparable with gold (Dyhrberg, 2016) or not (Klein et al., 2018). Over the years, Bitcoin has gained attention and market share. Another hacker published a white paper describing a blockchain of smart contracts called Ethereum (Buterin et al., 2014), which is a disruptive technology that can act as a digital, immutable, and programmable public agreement. This new technology has huge implications in mitigating informational asymme­ try, enhancing competition, and improving consumer welfare; yet, it may also lead to collusion (Cong and He, 2019). The appearance of altcoins provided an alternative investment op­ portunity. A good and popular example in Ethereum is an initial coin offering (ICO). An ICO acts as an initial public offering (IPO) of cryp­ tocurrencies worldwide. It serves as an early-stage crowdfunding method to raise funds for a new cryptocurrency before they are publicly traded (Gans and Catalini, 2018). Ethereum released the ERC20 feature, adding the possibility of creating new cryptocurrencies easily over the Ethereum blockchain. This led to an explosion of ICOs and signs of a bubble, but also great returns in compensation for the assumed risks (Benedetti and Kosto­ vetsky, 2021). This generated great volatility in the stock market (Akyildirim et al., 2020). ICOs grew exponentially from 2015 to the second quarter of 2018. The highest amount of USD 6 billion was raised in the first quarter of 2018. After that, sales dropped remarkably, E-mail address: j.pineiro@usc.es (J. Pi˜neiro-Chousa). Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Technological Forecasting & Social Change Received 25 August 2021; Received in revised form 4 May 2022; Accepted 8 May 2022

Technological Forecasting & Social Change 181 (2022) 121740 coinciding with the development of law enforcement actions in many countries worldwide. Thus, the exponential growth of ICOs accompa­ nied by the rise in problems related to security and investor protection led to increasingly concerned reactions by public and private regulators (Domingo et al., 2020). The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) started to consider tokens as a possible asset under securities regulation, in practice banning US citizens from investing (Hacker and Thomale, 2018). Security token offering (STO) appeared as an alternative to ICOs, because it complies with regional regulators, but the amount of capital raised by STOs is far lower than that of ICOs. Almost simultaneously, in the beginning of 2018, Bitcoin suffered depreciation. The depreciation of the value of Bitcoin, depreciation of ETH, and demise of the ICO market led to the so-called crypto winter. Finance professionals who used ICOs to develop Ethereum-based financial products led the emergence of the DeFi market in the begin­ ning of 2020. DeFi financial products built into Ethereum smart con­ tracts play a significant role in the development of the Ethereum network. It is believed that they will likely continue to play an increasingly significant role in the future (Wulf, 2020). The purpose of this research is to study the relationship between DeFi tokens, traditional assets, and user-generated content. DeFi is a very new technology, and very few studies based on volatility have been con­ ducted. Therefore, this is a preliminary study of this new field. We use a dataset comprising the daily returns of 13 DeFi, VIX, S&P GSCI Crude Oil Index, S&P GSCI Gold Index, and the daily variation of DeFi mentions in Telegram chats and Twitter, over the study period covering 1 July 2018–27 July 2020. After thoroughly describing the decentralised finance from different perspectives and angles in relation to traditional finance, this study aims to analyse the influence of the variables mentioned on DeFi returns. This study contributes to the advancement of knowledge on this novel topic by examining the impact of selected sentiment variables, such as Telegram chats and Twitter mentions, on DeFi returns. The results of this study can guide policymakers as well as public and private investors in their decisions towards more efficient This paper is structured as follows: Section 2 provides the theoretical framework and formulates the hypotheses; Section 3 describes the method, including the sample, variables, and model; Section 4 presents the main empirical results; Section 5 discusses the results; Section 6 presents the implications; and Section 7 provides the conclusion and From a broad perspective, DeFi refers to a recent disruptive process that promotes the use of blockchain technology, smart contracts, decentralised ledgers, and applications based on open protocols or modular frameworks for creating and issuing all kinds of financial products and services. Ethereum and other smart contract platforms allow any code to be run using blockchain technology. This opens the possibility of creating decentralised applications (Dapps), decentralised autonomous organisationsDecentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAOs), and decentralised financial services (DeFi). The financial and technical complexity is much higher than that of the ICOs based on ERC20, but the efforts, funding, and interest in the crypto community during these years seem to open a new and exciting possibility for The term decentralised finance is very wide because most blockchain projects are decentralised by definition, and any cryptocurrency (e.g. Bitcoin) can be categorised as a financial asset (Baur et al., 2018). In all its nuances, most of the literature considers DeFi as the replication of financial services over blockchain. Some authors provide a more precise categorisation of the services (Gudgeon et al., 2020; Sch¨ar, 2020; Katona 2021), while others have a broader focus (Chen and Bellavitis, 2020). Still, some others focus on specific interoperability services like oracles (Liu et al., 2020). Undoubtedly, the amount of money locked in DeFi smart contracts is growing exponentially, from USD 1 billion in June 2020, to USD 2 billion in July 2020, and to USD 4 billion in August 2020. The tradeable tokens behind the DeFi projects surpassed USD 10 billion in market capitalisation. In other words, the use of these new services seems to be exploding, together with the market value of or­ ganisations related to decentralised finance. 2.2. Decentralised financial services Lending and borrowing are services defined by a smart contract asking for, or offering, cryptocurrencies paying or earning an interest rate, respectively. Platforms such as Aave, Compound, Yearn finance, Just, or Kava offer these services in the form of loans or flash loans. Loans require borrowers to over guarantee their loans, allowing the lender to redeem pledged collateral in the event of default by the borrower. Flash loans allow borrowing without a guarantee because the atomicity of the smart contract protects the loaned amount. Therefore, a loan not repaid with interest means that the whole transaction will be reversed (Gudgeon et al., 2020; Sch¨ar, 2020). Flash loans are not easily comparable to traditional services because they rely on the power of smart contracts. For example, if someone with a small amount of money wants to buy a house and sell it immediately in the same operation, they could use a flash loan, avoiding the need to have all the money required by the operation. Although they are becoming popular for arbitrage operations, they have safety problems (Qin et al., 2020) because of at­ tacks related to oracle manipulation and the pump and arbitrage Stablecoins are crypto assets with a price-stabilization mechanism pegged to cryptocurrency, fiat money, or exchange-traded commodities. USDT and USDC are cryptocurrencies paired with a dollar but central­ ised, as each USDT or USDC is backed by a USD. When stablecoins are backed by another cryptocurrency and regulated by smart contracts, they are considered decentralised or DeFi. The most popular stablecoin is DAI. One DAI is equated to one USD and uses a stabilisation mecha­ nism to protect against volatility. In fact, to borrow DAIs, a user must pledge an over-guaranteed number of cryptocurrencies. When the price of DAI differs from that of its peg, arbitrageurs are incentivised to buy or sell DAI to compensate for supply and demand movements. If the collateral cryptocurrency devaluates in relation to the USD, the borrower must increase the amount of collateral to keep the ratio above some liquidation threshold; otherwise, the borrow position will be liquidated at a discount, and a penalty fee will be charged (Qin et al., Exchanges are key components of blockchain ecosystems. They allow changes between fiat and cryptocurrency, and are the main entrance of cryptocurrencies. Some of them offer more products, such as staking, options, or derivatives, and they have initial exchange offerings (IEOs), which is their own version of ICOs. IEOs are centralised, and their effi­ ciency is based on trust in the exchange operator, because to trade in them, traders must deposit assets in the exchange account, which can be accessed by the centralised exchanges. DeFi proposed decentralised exchange (DEX) as an alternative to centralised exchanges. In DEX, users maintain control of their assets instead of the exchange account. Trade execution occurs atomically through a smart contract in which both parties trade around one indivisible transaction (Sch¨ar, 2020). This mode of operation removes intermediaries and adds transparency, but it also demands interoperability with other services such as oracles or lending to have liquidity. Balancer, Uniswap, 0x, Kiber network, Bancor, and Loopring are examples of actual decentralised exchanges. Decentralised derivatives are tokens that derive their value from the performance of an underlying asset, the result of an event, or the behaviour of any other observable variable (Sch¨ar, 2020). Synthetix is a derivative liquidity protocol on Ethereum which allows for the issuance and trading of synthetic assets. Each synthetic asset (SYNTH) is an J. Pi˜neiro-Chousa et al.

Technological Forecasting & Social Change 181 (2022) 121740 ERC20 token that replicates external asset prices and uses smart contract infrastructure underpinned by the Synthetix Network Token (SNX) as collateral. Interested parties are rewarded for their support of the system with a proportional share of the fees generated by the activity. Conse­ quently, the SNX value is directly related to the use of the network it Oracles are systems that provide trustworthy information from the real world (off-chain) to blockchain (on-chain). They are not services for the end-user, but are crucial for the DeFi ecosystem. Oracles provide real-time information about assets used as collateral and redemptions, such as market prices. As price is very volatile, the security of many DeFi systems relies on oracles. An oracle introduces data into a smart contract from a third-party source, assuring veracity in different ways (Liu et al., 2020). The most successful project in this area was Chainlink (LINK). It raised money from an ICO in 2017 at a price of USD 0.11 per token, and at the time this paper was written (August, 2020), the price of each token was 150 times higher (USD 16.87). 2.4. DeFi objectives and challenges The main objective of decentralised finance is to provide financial services over the blockchain in a decentralised manner. As DeFi origi­ nated from blockchain systems, it seems that everything is already decentralised by default, but this is not true. Some operations still need to be performed by external agents, and users must trust them to use the service. Thus, DeFi can be understood as a step further in the decen­ Innovation is the key to achieving these services in a technical and economic sense. On the one hand, smart contract technology over blockchains (mainly Ethereum) and other platforms is a very novel technology that is still in the early stages of development. This tech­ nology congregates top-minded engineers working in a completely new field related to computer science, knowing that they have the huge task of improving the technology while maintaining compatibility, func­ tionality, and with an immutable history of data and code releases. On the other hand, economic innovation redefines traditional economy in a completely new ecosystem, changing the sense of value, trust, coin, equity, or lending. The impact of economic innovation and specifically of DeFi on traditional economies, companies, and financial decision- makers will, in turn, bring more innovation in this field. The implica­ tions need to be carefully analysed. Based on the experience with ICOs and their decline, countries’ regulations have had an important role that now DeFi wants to surpass. As everything is on-chain and regulated through smart contracts, ano­ nymity allows movement beyond the bounds of government regulations. DeFi may not be a success, but one of its main objectives is to be bor­ derless. The obsession with smart contracts arose from the pursuit of transparency by DeFi. In this sense, as a smart contract is an open-source code, immutable when executed on a blockchain (even creators cannot change it), DeFi projects act as ‘glass boxes’ as everybody can see inside. DeFi is sometimes defined as a layered structure composed of settlement (e.g. Ethereum), asset (e.g. ERC20), protocol (e.g. Uniswap), application (e.g. Uniswap interface), and aggregation (e.g. Uniswap used by the third party) (Sch¨ar, 2020). To make it possible for all these elements to interact properly, considering the technical complexity of smart con­ tracts, interoperability has become one objective to achieve in DeFi These objectives, namely innovation, decentralisation, border­ lessness, transparency, and interoperability (Chen and Bellavitis, 2020), act as a lever to push DeFi forward. DeFi seems to be a big step toward the decentralisation of value, but it faces many challenges that could limit its expansion. These challenges, which are also blockchain challenges, should be addressed in this new crypto project. These include fraud, volatility, usability, regulatory un­ certainty, privacy, building distributed trust, use of inflexible systems, lack of accountability, and lack of human presence (Chen and Bellavitis, 2020; Qin et al., 2020). Safety problems have already appeared and it is easy to predict that they will continue to appear in the future. They can be in any part of the software; however, the magnitude of the security systems determines their impact on the ecosystem. During the ICO rush, some scams and bad practices appeared, and although DeFi is more transparent, technical and financial innovations could open doors to fraud, which can result in a “cost of dishonesty” (Domingo et al., 2020). Another important chal­ lenge relates to transparency, as it has a counterpart in possible privacy problems. If everything is recorded on public ledgers, there is anonymity but not necessarily privacy. All of this bears on the future of any project based on cryptocurrencies as it is always under the threat of government regulations, for example, by banning or constraining their use, similar to what happened to ICOs. Other challenges refer to the fact that decen­ tralised financial services are consumed using digital applications, and it is common for a new software to lack usability, especially when dealing with very complex systems. In contrast, most DeFi cryptocurrencies are recent and have modest market caps, which can generate a lot of vola­ tility when a sudden interest or disinterest occurs. Finally, the use of smart contracts ensures that it is not possible to change what is executed, even if both parties agree to a change. This means that human judge­ ment does not count after a smart contract is released, which imposes important decision limitations. At this point, one question arises: if there is no central organisation responsible for the service, who is responsible for any error or failure? Fig. 1 2.5. DeFi as an investment opportunity A good way to measure how DeFi projects are progressing is to examine the behaviour of its tradeable tokens. Tradeable tokens, often called cryptocurrencies, are used for governance, utility, security, or a combination of these for a given crypto project. Interest in a crypto project could lead a token to undergo variations in supply or demand and hence, in its price. Moreover, because cryptocurrencies are financial assets, they could be related to traditional assets as a significant part of their portfolio stocks (Gil-Alana et al., 2020). Some authors compared major cryptocurrencies and found evidence of integration in the prices of some altcoins, similar to what happens with stock indices (Gil-Alana et al., 2020). Other authors have created a value index of crypto­ currencies that persistently cross-correlates with the Dow-Jones Indus­ trial Average (Zhang et al., 2018). Previous research compared the value of certain cryptocurrencies, such as Bitcoin and gold, reporting mixed results (Dyhrberg, 2016; Klein et al., 2018). Similarly, other studies suggest that there is a relationship between cryptocurrencies and broadly-defined commodity markets, such as energy, metals, and agri­ culture (Ji et al., 2019). More recently, a broader analysis based on the time and frequency domains found no relationship between the three largest cryptocurrencies and various other financial assets (Corbet et al., Accordingly, more research is needed, and the following hypothesis : Stock market volatility influences DeFi returns 2.6. Gold and oil in relation to crypto assets The relationship between cryptocurrencies and gold has been and still is a very controversial topic. Some studies have demonstrated a correlation between Bitcoin and gold (Dyhrberg, 2016), but others (Klein et al., 2018) have not observed this correlation. One of the main ideas behind these studies is the possible use of Bitcoin as a gold-like asset, which is investment-safe. However, cryptocurrencies are beyond Bitcoin. Some studies include other cryptocurrencies, such as Ethereum J. Pi˜neiro-Chousa et al.

Technological Forecasting & Social Change 181 (2022) 121740 or Ripple (Beneki et al., 2019) but focus on volatility spillovers or bear markets (Kyriazis et al., 2019). Studies on other individual crypto­ currencies or class-grouped cryptocurrencies are lacking. The same is true of the relationship between cryptocurrencies and oil. Al-Yahyaee et al. (2019) studied the relationship between the volatility of Bitcoin and oil markets, while Okorie and Lin (2020) focused on the volatility of some top cryptocurrencies and oil as a hedge for investors. Similarly, Yin et al. (2021) studied hedges, but in the particular case of oil market shocks. These studies showed a certain relationship between the volatility of oil and some cryptocurrencies, but the economic implications focused on hedges and portfolio manage­ ment. We were unable to find studies that compared gold, oil assets, and DeFi tokens. Therefore, the following hypotheses are proposed: : DeFi return is influenced by energy sources such as oil (S&P GSCI : A DeFi is a safe asset, and its return is positively associated with the returns on other safe assets such as gold (S&P GSCI Gold Index). 2.7. Role of user-generated content in crypto assets User-generated content (UGC) can be broadly materialised (e.g. wikis, evaluation websites, blogs, social media instant messaging apps) in different types of content formats (e.g. text, photo, videos, games) with countless purposes (e.g. entertainment, social-political campaigns, getting updated). Several researchers have analysed the diverse roles of UGC in the blockchain domain. Mai et al. (2018) used tweets and forum posts to forecast Bitcoin movement. Guske and Bendig (2018) found a relationship between the number of Twitter messages and the amount of funding raised. Rohr and Wright (2019) argued that the promoter or seller of a token often establishes communication centres (via slack, telegram, or message boards) to keep interested parties informed. Recently, Domingo et al. (2020) found that sentiment extracted from social networks positively influences ICO returns. To the best of our knowledge, no study has analysed the influence of UGC, namely, Tele­ gram and Twitter, on DeFi returns. Hence, the following hypotheses are : Public user-generated content in Twitter influences DeFi returns. : Private user-generated content in Telegram influences DeFi As previously mentioned, the crypto landscape has changed over time, starting with Bitcoin, followed by smart contracts and initial coin offerings. Currently, DeFi is the main focus of change. Their novelty and limited evaluation of implications can generate a lot of volatility, mainly if certain events trigger a huge interest or disinterest and, consequently, huge price movements. Therefore, it is necessary to carefully analyse the relationship between DeFi volatility and stock market volatility measured through the Chicago Board Options Exchange Market Fig. 1. DeFi’s services, objectives, and challenges. Source: Own elaboration. J. Pi˜neiro-Chousa et al.

Technological Forecasting & Social Change 181 (2022) 121740 Volatility Index to better understand DeFi behaviour. The mixed results obtained by previous research and the lack of studies analysing the re­ lationships between DeFi returns and stock market volatility or com­ modities such as gold or oil demand more scientific research. 3.1. Data and variables The database used in the analysis comprises the daily returns of DeFi, VIX, S&P GSCI crude oil index, S&P GSCI gold index, and the daily variation of DeFi mentions in Telegram chats and Twitter. The DeFi data, including social media mentions, were downloaded from Santi­ ment.net. Santiment.net is a website and a crypto-based project that gathers data about cryptocurrencies – price, development activity, de­ rivatives, or social media mentions, such as Twitter and Telegram. S&P GSCI Gold Index data were provided by the S&P Dow Jones Indices®. The VIX and S&P GSCI crude oil index data were downloaded from Initially, we obtained data from 29 DeFi. We drop a DeFi from the sample if they have been unlisted for a long time. We also excluded DeFi, which only began in 2019 or 2020. Finally, we used a dataset consisting of 13 DeFi with returns from 1 July 2018 to 27 July 2020. The daily returns of DeFi were calculated as: Rnit = (Pnit −Pnit−1)/Pnit−1 where Pnit is the closing price of DeFi at moment t. This method was chosen because several DeFi have a closing price between 0 and 1, which makes it impossible to calculate returns following Campbell et al. However, the daily returns of the VIX, S&P GSCI crude oil index, and S&P GSCI gold index were calculated following Campbell et al. Rit = ln(Pit) −ln(Pit−1) where Pit is the closing price of index i (VIX, S&P GSCI Crude Oil Index, or S&P GSCI Gold Index) at moment t. VIX, S&P GSCI Crude Oil Index, and S&P GSCI Gold Index have trading days without trade (Saturday and Sunday), but DeFi has trades in those days. Thus, we suppose that VIX, S&P GSCI Crude Oil Index, and S&P GSCI Gold Index have the same closing prices on days without trade as the last day with trade (Friday). The daily variation of the mentions was calculated as follows: TEit = (TEit −TEit−1)/TEit−1 TWit = (TWit −TWit−1)/TWit−1 where TEit is the number of mentions in the Telegram chats of DeFi i at moment t, and TWit is the number of mentions in Twitter of DeFi i at Descriptive statistics for panel data with 9854 observations are presented in Table 1. The references in Telegram chats and Twitter have a lower number of observations because there are no mentions every day and because of the daily variation calculation. DeFi returns are repre­ sented by a dichotomous variable, where ‘1′ is for positive or zero returns, and ‘0′ for negative returns. To measure the relationship between assets in a nonlinear way, we use a logit-probit model, a widely used econometric method (Lennox, 1999). This nonlinear methodology has been applied to stocks and volatility by Thomas et al. (2014) and many others. We will take into consideration the results in the same way as other authors have done This method allows us to test how financial market movements in­ fluence DeFi returns. The following logit-probit model was proposed: Rnit = β0 + β1Rvit + β2Rcit + β3Rgit + β4TEit + β5TWit + εt where Rnit represents the daily returns of DeFi (dichotomous variable), Rvit is the daily return of VIX, Rcit represents the daily returns of the S&P GSCI crude oil index, Rgit is the daily returns of the S&P GSCI Gold Index, TEit is the daily variation of mentions in Telegram chats, and TWit rep­ resents the daily variation of mentions on Twitter. Both probit and logit models were estimated using a population- averaged estimator. This means that the β measures the change in pro­ portion to y = 1 for a unit increase in x (Neuhaus et al., 1991). Table 2 shows the correlations between the variables. The VIX and variation in Twitter mentions are negatively correlated with DeFi returns. This suggests that when volatility increases, DeFi returns decrease, and vice versa. In contrast, Twitter and Telegram chat refer­ ences are positively correlated to DeFi returns. That is, an increase in Twitter or Telegram mentions could cause an increase in DeFi returns. Furthermore, the correlations between the S&P GSCI crude oil index returns and S&P GSCI gold index returns are positive, suggesting that an increase in these indices could cause an increase in DeFi returns. These suggest that DeFi movements are similar to market movements. Table 3 shows the logit-probit results for the whole sample. The re­ sults were similar for both regressions, with coefficient estimates for all variables except the S&P GSCI Crude Oil Index returns and the daily variation of Twitter mentions are significant. DeFi reacts inversely to market volatility (VIX), moves in the same direction as gold, while the coefficient estimate of gold is significantly higher than the coefficient estimate of VIX. Results also suggest a strong correlation between DeFi and the references in Telegram. Hausman’s test showed that best fit was obtained with the probit J. Pi˜neiro-Chousa et al.

Technological Forecasting & Social Change 181 (2022) 121740 The results suggest that DeFis can act as a safe haven, like gold. As shown by the positive correlation and significance of the logit-probit model estimates, parallelism with gold in terms of volatility is a hot topic in crypto research. Some authors such as Dyhrberg (2016) support it, while others such as Klein et al. (2018) do not. Both use GARCH methods, so this could be a methodology for future research. Indeed, our results are significant, but not in the higher range of significance, so other methodologies could help support hypothesis H3. Following the seminal article by Whaley (2000), which outlined the methodology used in calculating VIX, Diavatopoulos et al., (2007) confirmed that implied volatility is superior to realized volatility in determining future returns. Interestingly, it has also been shown that the VIX is influenced by a range of characteristics demonstrated by non-technical investors in social media (L´opez-Cabarcos et al., 2017). As implied volatility is our main interest, the VIX is an important variable to check if traditional stock volatility could be related to DeFi volatility. Our correlation and logit-probit model results support the inverse rela­ tionship, because the decrease in DeFi causes an increase in VIX, and vice versa. Our results are contrary to the findings of Lopez-Cabarcos et al. (2021) on a positive causality between VIX and returns on Bitcoin, and are more attuned to findings that VIX has a negative impact on risk premiums (Durand et al., 2011) or returns on stock indices (Giot, 2005). Here, we used the S&P GSCI Gold Index to track the COMEX gold fu­ tures. While it is widely tradeable, its close relationship with spot prices is not affected (Jena et al., 2018). The positive and statistically signifi­ cant coefficient estimate of the gold proxy implies that Dyhrberg’s (2016) claims that Bitcoin is positioned between gold, as a store value, and the US currency, and hence, can be put forward as a hedging in­ strument, could also be partially applied to DeFi. H1 and the more sig­ nificant H3 seem to reinforce the idea of a safe haven for DeFi tokens. The GSCI crude oil index showed no significant influence on the studied DeFi, indicating that H2 was not proven. Other studies comparing the influence between oil assets and Bitcoin have shown that the production of oil positively influences Bitcoin (Kaabia et al., 2020), or support the idea of Bitcoin as part of a diversified portfolio due to its low correlation with commodities, especially oil (Symitsi and Chalvat­ zis, 2019). The different time frames and methods make the comparison The relationship between volatility and user-generated content shows that the daily variation of Telegram chats mentions influence DeFi returns positively (H4). Contrary to Pinero-Chousa et al. (2021), we find that the influence of Twitter mentions is insignificant (H5). These results are in line with other studies (L´opez-Cabarcos et al., 2017), which show the influence of UGC and volatility depending on the type of investor and social network. In this study, Telegram chat groups of the DeFi project are more appealing to investors and people interested in the project. Contrastingly, Twitter is a more open platform for people with close interest in this type of project. As this is a subjective appreciation, more research can be conducted on the influence of UGC on DeFi. As was mentioned, this study makes an important contribution to the extant body of knowledge by examining the impact of selected sentiment variables on DeFi returns. Telegram chats support our claim, contrary to some previous studies, whereas Twitter is not a potential tool for trading in DeFi. While commodity markets and stock market vola­ tility are relevant in this study, the S&P 500 Index is not an important variable in determining DeFi returns. DeFi can be analysed from three perspectives: DeFi as an alternative investment asset, the communication channels of DeFi, and the utility/ In a very low or negative interest rate environment, investors are forced to take more risks or look for safe havens. Cryptocurrencies are a risky investment. However, this study found that gold influences DeFi, and VIX inversely influences DeFi. This makes DeFi a hedge against the volatility of the stock market and a safe haven. Consequently, these assets are useful in investment portfolios to balance risk and volatility. We live in the information era, and everybody can write anything on any web or platform on the internet. Telegram is a private communi­ cation channel that usually focuses on one topic, whereas Twitter is a public and multi-topic channel. According to the results of this study, information from private and topic-focused platforms is more relevant for DeFi projects in terms of its correlation with token prices. As most crypto projects are present in both channels, this study helped assess The main objective of DeFi is to remove middlemen from financial services. Its adoption has exploded and it seems to be just starting. Depending on the implications of the investment and communication perspective, DeFi can be considered either an opportunity or a threat to the existing financial system. 7. Conclusion and future research In terms of volatility, we analyse the influence of some traditional assets on some very specific crypto assets–decentralised finance tokens. As they are quite new, we thoroughly discuss these assets in the litera­ ture review. These results lead us to conclude that: (1) VIX inversely influences DeFi, (2) GSI crude oil has no influence on DeFi, and (3) the gold index influences DeFi, prompting us to claim that DeFi acts as a safe haven. This study can be added to a very broad and debated topic, and the alignment with methodologies and timeframes of other studies could be part of future research for better comparison. The results obtained in user content generation are as follows: (4) Telegram chats influence DeFi and, (5) Twitter activity does not influ­ ence DeFi. As both assets and UGC platforms change significantly over time, the conclusion should be taken as specific to this case, requiring a broader study in terms of time, assets, and platforms. As a general conclusion and taking into account that the technologies of DeFi assets are in their infancy, we can confirm there are relationships amongst some traditional assets, user-generated content, and DeFi. This influence may imply that DeFi tokens are safe havens, hedges for stock market volatility, and are linked to activity in Telegram’s private The success of decentralised finance has attracted widespread in­ terest from investors. Growth during the last months can be a sign for investors with a risk appetite looking for significant profit and a safe haven, or be included as high-risk investment instruments of a diversi­ fied portfolio. This should also be a warning for policymakers trying to avoid the situation that the ICO created several years ago. This situation of considerable returns from weak-value propositions leads to a rush of projects that lack solid foundations, or are even scams. As DeFi seems to have linked properties to some traditional investments and has the goal of recreating traditional finance services, we strongly recommend ana­ DeFi is a novel product with limited dataset availability, and changes in the regulatory environment can have an impact on out-of-sample *** significance at 1% level; ** significance at 5% level; * significance at 10% J. Pi˜neiro-Chousa et al.

Technological Forecasting & Social Change 181 (2022) 121740 results. Thus, the sample size is affected by the lack of longer time-series data. In this study, we include data from the pre-COVID 19 period, and the results could vary if analysed during and after the current health crisis. With an increase in the number of DeFi, a larger sample size is possible in the future and illiquidity issues can be addressed more meticulously. It is also advisable to include the variations in current sentiment variables and examine the introduction of new ones with the advancement of AI. In future research, it will be necessary to examine not only the COVID-19 period, but also the post-pandemic period. In summary, the DeFi landscape of projects and their behaviour as a category of crypto assets is rising and may change significantly in the future. This demands much more research effort. Research in International Business and Finance 62 (2022) 101699 Available online 9 June 2022 0275-5319/© 2022 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved. An intra-cryptocurrency analysis of volatility connectedness and its determinants: Evidence from mining coins, non-mining coins Lanouar Charfeddine a, Noureddine Benlagha a,*, Karim Ben Khediri b a Department of Finance and Economics, College of Business and Economics, Qatar University, P.O. Box 2713, Doha, Qatar b University of Carthage, FSEGN, University of Tunis El Manar, International Finance Group Tunisia and CEROS, University Paris Nanterre, France A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T This paper analysis the dynamic of the volatility connectedness between three main categories of the cryptocurrency market (mineable coins, non-mineable coins and tokens).1 Furthermore, it investigates factors (internal and external) that can help to predict the net directional and total volatility connectedness indices. We find that the CC market is highly connected where mining coins dominate the CC market and are typically net transmitter of shocks. For the non-mining coins, a low connectedness with all mining coins and tokens versus a high connectedness with Stellar are highlighted. The results show also that, unlike previous studies, Ethereum is the dominant contributor to volatility spillover and the only CC net pairwise transmitter in our sample. Interestingly, we find that most of the tokens are highly connected with their platform (Ethereum) rather than bitcoin. Finally, we find that both financial and macroeconomic in­ dicators play an important role in explaining the total and net directional connectedness, a result that can significantly help cryptocurrency market participants and investors improve their risk management and the benefits from diversification. Over the last few years, CCs2 have attracted the interest of several types of market participants including miners, investors, portfolio managers, academic researchers and policy makers. The specific features and characteristic of CCs such as their high returns and high time varying volatilities are the main motives behind this (Corbet et al., 2020; Yousaf and Ali, 2021; Mariana et al., 2021; Maouchi et al., 2021; Montasser et al., 2021). In fact, beyond the high level of returns that can offer CCs, investors have been also interested by E-mail addresses: lcharfeddine@qu.edu.qa (L. Charfeddine), nbenlagha@qu.edu.qa (N. Benlagha), karim.benkhediri@fsegn.ucar.tn 1 The list four mining coins considered in this study contains Bitcoin (BTC), Ethereum (ETH), Litecoin (LTC) and DASH (DASH), the four non- mining coins are Ripple (XRP), EOS (EOS), Binance coin (BNB), and Stellar (XLM)), and the list of four tokens includes Maker (MKR), Basic Attention (BAT), Chainlink (LINK), Augur (REP)). 2 The cryptocurrency market has witnessed a real boom in terms of the number of cryptocurrencies (CCs), number of CCs wallets and market capitalization. For instance, starting with Bitcoin as the only cryptocurrency in 2009, the total number of CCs has surpassed 17 277 by February 2022. Moreover, according to blockchain.com website, the number of CCs wallets has increased from 2 wallets in November 29, 2011 to 81 million wallets in February 2022. Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Research in International Business and Finance Received 25 August 2021; Received in revised form 29 May 2022; Accepted 31 May 2022

Research in International Business and Finance 62 (2022) 101699 the financial opportunities that can generate these new types of assets (digital assets) such as financial diversification and hedging strategies (Corbet et al., 2018, 2020; Charfeddine et al., 2020; Yousaf and Ali, 2021; Mariana et al., 2021; Maouchi et al., 2021). Empirically, while the number of research studies investigating the financial aspects (stylized facts, intra- and inter-dependence, and financial diversification) has exploded only recently that academic researchers have been interested to explore the level of financial connectedness involving CCs (Yi et al., 2018; Koutmos, 2018; Ji et al., 2019a). This paper try to contribute to this gap in the digital financial literature by investigating the dynamic features of connectedness between different categories of CCs. Moreover, it examines both internal and external factors that can help to predict the total and net directional connectedness indices. An important motivation of this study is the absence of a common classification of different types of CCs. For instance, according to Hardle et al. (2020), cryptocurrencies may be grouped into seven broad classes. The first class, originally designed as a transaction mechanism, which include for example Bitcoin and Litecoin. Ethereum, Tezos, EOS, and DFinity fall into the second class known as a distributed computation token. The third class of cryptocurrency is called a utility token, which is a programmable blockchain asset (for example, Golem, Storj, Sia and FileCoin). The fourth class of cryptocurrency is a security token. The fifth class is called fungible tokens (for example, ERC-20). A nonfungible token is the sixth classification. In this case, each token is unique and not interchangeable with another (For example, Cryptokitties and Decentraland (LAND). The final class of crypto­ currencies is called stablecoins (For example, tether (USDT), Circle’s USDC, LBXPeg). In this paper, we adopt a more general classification approach as we categorize all cryptocurrnecies into two main categories namely: (1) coins, and (2) tokens. In addition, we divide the coin category into two sub-categories namely mining coins (such as Bitcoin (BTC), Ethereum (ETH), DASH (DASH), Litecoin (LTC)) and the non-mining coins (such as Ripple (XRP), Binance Coin (BNB), EOS (EOS), Stellar (XLM)). Regarding the tokens category and compared to coins, the interest in this second category can be explained by the fact that they have been only recently emerged. The well known and most traded tokens include: Maker (MKR), Basic Attention (BAT), Chainlink (LINK), and Augur (REP). Despite that all the CCs (coins and tokens) share some important common features such as the blockchain technology, there exist also some other important characteristics that can distinguish coins from tokens and vis-versa. For instance, coins are primary designed for purpose of decentralized peer-to-peer payments.3 The main differences between the mineable and non-mineable coins is that contrary to the non-mining coins where the supply of coins is managed by the company offering these coins, the supply for the case of mining coins is endogenously determined by the mining process. The mining process is the process where the miners (individuals or companies) are rewarded when they validate the mining coin transaction. Yet, the tokens are a new form of crowdfunding that offer tokens to investors with the only potential gain is the increase in the price of tokens. Token offerings are smart contracts programmed on distributed ledger technology (Momtaz, 2020). Furthermore, tokens are applications built upon existing blockchains, whereas coins are created on their own blockchain (Momtaz, 2019). For further information on the market evolution, offering mechanisms, and token Garman & Klass Volatility 3 The main advantages of coins as instruments of payments are their ability to ensure anonymity, fast speed of transactions via the peer-to-peer transactions system, and lower costs compared to traditional payment system, e.g. fiat currency. L. Charfeddine et al.

Research in International Business and Finance 62 (2022) 101699 types, see the paper by Momtaz (2019). This study has two main objectives. First, it analyzes the intra-cryptocurrency market volatility spillovers. Second, it investigates whether macroeconomic and financial indicators can help in predicting the dynamic behavior of the net directional and total volatility connectedness indices. Several reasons motivate the focus on this research question. Firstly, while there have been a plenty of studies that have tried to explore the level of connectedness between the CCs and traditional financial assets (Corbet et al., 2018; Koutmos, 2018; Yi et al., 2018; Ji et al., 2019a; Billah et al., 2022), only few have analyzed the connectedness between different type of CCs. Secondly, the recent rapid gaining of interest of non-mining coins and tokens in the last two years has question the dominance and influential position of the mining coins in term of net transmitter/receiver volatility spillover. Thirdly, the recent emergence of a large set of tokens (such as DeFi, decentralized finance) offer some new other potential benefits for markets participants but open new questions, including whether Ethereum, as blockchain platform for almost all the tokens4 will be the more influential in terms of net transmitter/ receiver spillover. Finally, to the best of our knowledge, this is the first work that attempts to fill these gaps by exploring the relationship and volatility connectedness between different CCs types (coins versus tokens, and mining versus non-mining for coins). Our study classify the different CCs based on their principal characteristics of coins versus tokens, and of mining versus non-mining for the coins. In addition, we explore whether the tokens platforms influence their volatility connectedness. A further motivation is the lack of studies that have investigated the common factors that can explain the dynamic spillovers between different digital assets. Thus, in addition to the evaluation of the spillovers between the considered cryptocurrencies, this study aims to identify factors (internal to CC market, macroeconomic, financial of the dynamic connectedness cryptocurrencies. We first investigate the determinants of total connectedness. Then, we extend our analysis by investigating the factors affecting the net connectedness for each single cryptocurrency. In this paper, we report several key findings. First, mining coins dominate the CC market and are typically net transmitter of shocks while the tokens are mostly followers. Secondly, the non-mining coins exhibit a low connectedness with all mining coins and tokens versus a high connectedness with Stellar are highlighted. Further, unlike previous studies, we found that Ethereum is the dominant contributor to volatility spillover and the only CC net pairwise transmitter in our sample. More interestingly, our findings reveal that most of the tokens are highly connected with their platform (Ethereum) rather than bitcoin. Finally, the findings of our paper indicate that several financial and macroeconomic factors significantly contribute in the dynamics the total and net directional connectedness. The rest of the paper is organized as follow. Section 2 briefly reviews the relevant literature. Section 3 introduces the materials and methods. Section 4 presents and discusses the results and finally Section 5 concludes. In the last few years, a large number of studies in the finance literature has been devoted to studying financial connectedness (returns and volatility) among various assets types. The main motives is their important implications on portfolios performance (Diebold and Yilmaz, 2012, 2014). Recently, a special attention has been attributed to studying connectedness involving digital financial assets. The growing interest on digital financial assets was mainly due to the high financial returns of this new class of financial assets and the financial opportunities that can offer for investors and market participants in terms of hedging and portfolios diversification (Akhtaruzzaman et al., 2019; Bouri et al., 2017; Guesmi et al., 2019; Platanakis and Urquhart, 2019; Wang et al., 2019; Charfeddine et al., 2020). The overall connectedness literature involving digital financial assets can be regrouped into two strands of literature: (1) connectedness between traditional financial assets and digital assets; and (2) connectedness involving only digital financial assets. The first strand of literature concerns studies investigating how Bitcoin is correlated with traditional financial assets and alternative in­ vestments, global and emerging equity (Kajtazi and Moro, 2019), stock market indices (Benlagha, 2020; Benlagha and El Omari, 2022; Gil-Alana et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019), implied volatility of financial markets (Akyildirim et al., 2019), industry sectors and bond index (Akhtaruzzaman et al., 2019; Benlagha and Hemrit, 2022), a variety of other financial assets, including stocks, fixed income assets, energy and precious metals (Benlagha and Mseddi, 2019; Charfeddine et al., 2020; Corbet et al., 2018; Okorie and Lin, 2020). Note that most of these studies have focused on a single cryptocurrency market, notably the Bitcoin market as it is the most matured and liquid crypto market. In fact, academic researchers have focused much more on the Bitcoin than on others CCs, although the gap Another strand in the connectedness literature that emerged more recently has focused on the cross-cryptocurrency markets (Koutmos, 2018; Yi et al., 2018; Ji et al., 2019a; Balli et al., 2019). Katsiampa (2019a) provides evidence that time-varying conditional correlations exist between Ether and Bitcoin. This study is extended by Katsiampa (2019b) to include other CCs such as Ripple, Litecoin, and Stellar. Findings show that conditional correlations between different pairs of CCs are dynamic. Another closely related paper is Aslanidis et al. (2019) who document that conditional correlations among CCs (Bitcoin, DASH, Monero, and Ripple) are positive and varying across time. Katsiampa et al. (2019b) identify bi-directional volatility spillover effects between all the three pairs of Bitcoin, Ether and Litecoin and show existence of time-varying conditional correlations with positive correlations mostly prevailing. These findings are corroborated by the results of Katsiampa et al. (2019a) using intra-day data for eight CCs. In the same vein, Canh et al. (2019) find that volatility spillovers exist with strong positive correlations among seven largest CCs? examine the volatility spillover between Bitcoin, Ethereum, and Litecoin using the VAR-AGARCH model. They find that the conditional correlations between 4 Approximately 90% of the 1450 tokens traded based on coinmarketcap.com use the Ethereum Platform. Among the top 100 tokens only 9 tokens L. Charfeddine et al.

Research in International Business and Finance 62 (2022) 101699 all pairs of cryptocurrencies are observed to be higher during the COVID-19 period compared to the pre-COVID-19 period. Relying on Diebold and Yilmaz (2014) framework of variance decomposition, another set of studies investigating the connect­ edness of CCs document higher interdependencies between CCs, suggesting that CCs are becoming more integrated. For instance, Koutmos (2018) provides evidence that Bitcoin is the most influential regarding to volatility spillovers in the CC market. Further, results indicate that the intensity of these spillovers has risen steadily over time, due to a higher level of market uncertainty. Relatedly, Yi et al. (2018) find that the level of connectedness among CCs is high and fluctuate cyclically. Their results also reveal that CCs with big capitalizations are more probable to propagate volatility shocks to others. Similarly, Balli et al. (2019) find that the magnitude of short-term connectedness among CCs is much higher than medium and long term, and this connectedness is negatively related to economic uncertainty. Same results are found by Ciaian et al. (2018) applying the ARDL model to examine interdependencies between Bitcoin and Altcoin markets: the relationship is more pronounced in the short-run. Interestingly, Ji et al. (2019a) examine connect­ edness across six large CCs, by employing the approach of Diebold and Yilmaz (2012, 2015). They find that Bitcoin is the dominant contributor of volatility spillovers, followed by Litecoin. More recently, Antonakakis et al. (2019) find that Ethereum is the number one net transmitting cryptocurrency, followed by Bitcoin. They also show that the degree of connectedness is positively associated with Borri (2019) finds,by estimating the conditional tail-risk, that Bitcoin, Ether, Ripple and Litecoin are highly correlated. In a somewhat similar vein, Xu et al. (2020) obtain similar results for 23 CCs, and also find that the total connectedness increases steadily over time. Their results further reveal that Ethereum is the largest systemic risk emitter, while Bitcoin is the largest systemic risk receiver. In the same line, Tiwari et al. (2020) examine the dependence and contagion risk between Bitcoin, Litecoin and Ripple. Their results provide evidence of significant risk contagion among price returns of major CCs, both in bear and bull markets. On the contrary, Zieba et al (2019) find that variations in the price of Bitcoin do not affect and are not affected by variations in prices of other CCs. Using the multifractal analysis to investigate the herding biases, Mnif et al. (2020) quantify the self-similarity intensity of cryptocurrency (Bitcoin, Ethereum, Ripple, Litecoin and Binance) returns’ during the COVID-19 pandemic and report evidence that COVID-19 has a positive impact on the cryptocurrency market efficiency. In a similar vein, Vidal-Tomas (2021) uses a network approach to identify the transitions of the cryptocurrency market over time, and observes that COVID-19 significantly affected cryptocurrencies during a short period of financial panic, giving rise to a remarkable increase of market synchronization for Bitcoin, Ethereum, Ripple and Litecoin. All in all, the broad picture emerging from this literature is that CCs are highly correlated, and the total connectedness increases steadily over time, suggesting that market integration is a continuing and persistent phenomenon. Furthermore, although much effort has been devoted to examining the cross-correlations between CCs, little attention has been paid to the volatility connectedness or spillover effects among different type of CCs. Thus, our study using the spillover index approach to analyze the volatility connectedness across mining coins, non-mining coins and tokens will fill this gap. 3. Materials and methods To analyze the volatility connectedness between the twelve most traded cryptocurrencies reflecting the three categories of mining coins, non-mining coins and the tokens, we propose to use the Diebold and Yilmaz (2014, 2015) framework. Our choice of the Diebold and Yilmaz (2014, 2015) approach is mainly motivated by its several advantages when compared to other approaches (Otranto, 2015). For instance, beyond fulfilling our main goal of assessing the connectedness levels and directions among the digital assets under investigation, this approach allows the investigation of bilateral connectedness as well as displaying the strength and the dynamic of volatility connectedness. Furthermore, the method allows accurate comparisons among alternative model structures and variable sets. More importantly, compared with concurrent correlation-based methods such as the Wavelet analysis and the multivariate GARCH models, Costa et al. (2021) suggest that Diebold and Yilmaz (2012) methodology is advantageous as it enables inferring direction of spillovers for a wide number of simultaneously interacting variables in a clear and compact way. However, the major shortcoming of the method is that the estimated results might be influenced by the chosen window size. To overcome this limitation, the order of the estimated VAR should be well specified. Furthermore, a robustness check methodology should be appropriately conducted. In this section, we will follow and use the same notations for the connectedness measures as in the original paper of Diebold and Yilmaz The starting point of Diebold and Yilmaz (2012, 2014) analysis is to consider an N-dimensional vector (y1, y2, ..., yN) modeled by a covariance stationary vector autoregression VAR(p) model as, with ϵt ~ N(0, Σϵ) is a vector of an i. i. d, p is the order of the VAR model which is determined based on the information criteria and Φi for i = 1, 2, . . . p is the matrix of the model coefficients. The moving average representation of Eq. (1) is given by, where the N × N coefficients of the Ai matrices follow the recursion Ai = ∑p j=1ΦiAi−j, with A0 = IN and Ai = 0 for i < 0. The moving average representation is a key of the dynamic of the system under investigation since it allows the calculation of the variance de­ compositions. This variance decomposition splits the H – step-ahead forecast error variances of each variable of the model into L. Charfeddine et al.

Research in International Business and Finance 62 (2022) 101699 different parts attributable to the various system shocks. Diebold and Yilmaz (2012, 2014) propose to use the generalized variance decomposition (GVD) approach developed by Koop et al. (1996) and Pesaran and Shin (1998). The use of the GVD approach is mainly motivated by its ability to overcome the problem of variables ordering (Koop et al., 1996 and Pesaran and Shin, 1998).Following Diebold and Yilmaz (2014, 2015) the j’s contribution to variable i’s H – step-ahead generalized forecast error variance decomposition here σjj is the standard deviation of the error term for the jth equation. Σϵ represents the error vector, ϵ, covariance matrix. ej is the selected vector which has 1 at the jth element and zeros elsewhere. Ah are the coefficients as defined in the moving average repre­ sentation.Finally, to facilitate interpretation and comparability, each entry of of the GVD matrix is normalized by the row sum. Explicitly,the normalized entries are given by, As a consequence of this normalization, ∑N ij = 1 and ∑N Diebold and Yilmaz (2012, 2014) propose several measures of volatility connectedness that we group into three main categories based on the type of connectedness (single, total and grand total): (1) own, pairwise and net pairwise directional connectedness, (2) total directional connectedness “from" and “to", (3) net total directional connectedness, and finally (4) the total connectedness index. These four measures of connectedness are summarized in the table below: 3.1.1. Own, pairwise and net pairwise directional connectedness The upper-left block of Table 1 reports both the own-connectedness (diagonal entries) and the pairwises directional connectedness from cryptocurrency i to j and from j to i (off-diagonal entries). To calculate the net pairwise directional connectedness, one can just calculate the difference between the two paiwrise directional connectedness. These four individuals connectedness are defined as • The own-connectedness is defined by CH • The pairwise directional connectedness from j to i is defined by CH • The pairwise directional connectendess from i to j is defined by CH • The net pairwise directional connectendess from cryptocurrency i to currency j is defined as CH 3.1.2. Total directional connectedness “from others", “to others" and the net The two total directional connectedness “from others" and “to others" are also known as the total directional connectedness “received" and “transmitted", respectively. The total directional connectedness are defined as the share of the H-step forecast error variance of one variable coming from shocks arising in all the other variables. As shown in Table 1, there are 2N total directional connectedness, an N total directional connectedness “received" and N total directional connectedness “transmitted". As for the own and pairwise directional connectedness, the net total directional connectedness is calculated as the difference between the two “from others" and “to others" total directional connectedness. These three total directional connectedness can be defined as, • Total connectedness “from others" is defined by CH ij ,j ∕= i. • Total connectedness “to others" is defined by CH ij ,i ∕= j. • Total net connectedness is defined as CH 3.1.3. Total connectedness index (TCI) The total connectedness index is the grand total of the off-diagonal entries of the upper-left block of Table 1. This total connect­ edness measure is viewed as a systemic risk measure (Diebold and Yilmaz, 2012, 2014). Analytically, it is defined by, • TCI = CH ij ∕N,i ∕= j. L. Charfeddine et al.

Research in International Business and Finance 62 (2022) 101699 Two measures of market volatility will be used in this study: (1) the Garman and Klass (1980) measure and (2) the Parkinson (1980) measure. While, the results of the first measure will be discussed in details in the paper, the results for the second measure will be only presented as for robustness checking.5 The Garman and Klass (1980) measure is defined by, GKt = 0.511(Ht −Lt)2 −0.019[(Ct −Ot)(Ht + Lt −2Ot) −2(Ht −Ot)(Lt −Ot)] −0.383(Ct −Ot)2 where H, L, O, and C denote the high, low, opening and closing prices at day t, respectively. 4. Results and discussion 4.1. Data and variable description Daily high, low, opening and closing prices are used to calculate the Garman and Klass (1980) and Parkinson (1980) volatility series for the twelve considered cryptocurrencies. All the data was retrieved from https://coinmarketcap.com. Among the thousands of cryptocurrencies available for trading, we focus our analysis on the top CCs in terms of market capitalization and data availability. Precisely, the list of selected cryptocurrencies was subject to two major criteria: (1) we restrict our analysis on the top four mining coins and top four non-mining coins, and top four tokens in terms of market capitalization, and (2) the sample period used should include the last recent period of cryptocurrencies prices boom and bust (end of 2017 and beginning 2018). Moreover, for reason of data avail­ ability for the four tokens, we have been restricted to focus our analysis on the period from October 28th, 2017 to April 5th, 2020, which give us a total number of observations of T = 891. The final list of twelve CCs include: (1) the top four mining coins namely Bitcoin (BTC), Ethereum (ETH), Litecoin (LTC) and DASH (DASH), (2) the top four non-mining coins which includes Ripple (XRP), EOS (EOS), Binance coin (BNB), and Stellar (XLM)), and (3) finally the list of the top four tokens that includes Maker (MKR), Basic Attention (BAT), Chainlink (LINK), Augur (REP) (Hardle et al., 2020; Maouchi et al., 2021; Montasser et al., 2021). In terms of market capitalization, the twelve CC considered in this study account for more than 81% of the whole CC market as of April 5th, 2020. In our sample, the market value of the mining coins is $145.13b US, which exceeds the 90.9% of the whole market value of the twelve CCs. The non-mining coins and the tokens market values are $13.07b and $1.41b, which represent approximately 8.1% and approximately 1% of the total market value of the twelve CCs, respectively. This clearly shows the dominance of the cryptocurrency market by the mining coins where the sole Bitcoin market share exceeds the 64.4% of the total CC market (more than 5400 cryptocurrencies by the end of April 5th, 2020). Table 2 lists some other important characteristics of the twelve cryptocurrencies. The most attractive feature is the high variability of CC prices. An analysis by category shows that during our period of analysis, the prices of the mining coins have been multiplied by 285 times for Bitcoin and 5002 times for DASH. For non-mining coins prices have been multiplied between 44 times for EOS and 1127 times for Ripple. Finally, for tokens, prices have been multiplied by 10 times for ChainLink and 74 times for Maker. In terms of ranking, seven out of the twelve cryptocurrencies are ranked among the top 10 crypto-market. Only the four tokens are ranked above the top 20 Fig. 1 displays the volatility series by CCs category (mining coins, non-mining coins and tokens). We remark that all the three sub- figures reported in Fig. 1 show presence of extremes in the daily volatilities of most of the studied cryptocurrencies. For instance, the high volatility observed in 2017 is mainly due to the announcement of the US regulatory to reject the Bitcoin ETF in February 2017. In addition, targeting the Ethereum platform by cyber-attack in September 2017 led to a sharp increase in the volatility of most of the digital assets. However, the rapid increase in the BTC price notably between August ($2797) and December ($19 050), which cor­ responds to a rise in BCT price of approximately 600%, coincides with the Tweets by John McAfee in November 2017 that Bitcoin will reach $1 million by 2020. On the contrary, the value of BTC shows a decreasing trend between January and December 2018. We can offer possible explanations behind the sharp drop in prices from $ 10 823 to $3429. Finally, among the tokens, we find that Maker has the lowest correlation and that BAT has the highest correlation In the next section, we analyze the market volatility connectedness between the three cryptocurrency categories (intra-crypto­ currency). We start our analysis by discussing the results of the static connectedness based on the different connectedness measures presented in Section 3.1. Then, in the second step, we deeper the analysis by investigating the dynamic connectedness using the rolling window approach. Finally, in the last step, we explore and examine the possible determinants of both the net and total connectedness. 4.2.1. Own, pairwise and net pairwise directional connectedness To assess how volatility shocks are transmitted across cryptocurrencies, we begin our analysis by focusing on the “individual" connectedness measures. The results matrix of static connectedness for the twelve CCs are reported in Table 4 for the GK volatility measure. Several important features can be drawn from the table. First, the results show that the own connectedness elements are the largest individual elements of the table. On average, tokens possess the largest own connectedness, followed by non-mining coins and finally mining coins. Precisely, the results show that the own-effects (own share) for the mining coins range between 19.58% for Ethereum and 27.08% for the DASH coin. For the non-mining coins, the own-effects range between 26.42% for EOS and 36.38% for XRP (Ripple). Finally, for the tokens, the own-connectednesse range between 40.44% for LINK and 56.77% for MKR coin to 81.08% for the BAT coin. Comparing our results of own-effects to some recent published articles, we find that the own connectedness is much lower than Ji et al. (2019a) who find values of own connectedness raging from 46.7% for Litecoin to 97.1% for DASH for their 07/08/2015 - 22/02/2018 sample and range between 37% to 60.8% for the 01/01/2017- 22/02/2018 subsample. There are several possible explanations for this high difference in results: (1) the cryptocurrency market becomes more integrated over time inducing an Fig. 2. This figure reports on top the (absolute) value of the correlation and the result of the correlation test is given by stars. On bottom, the bivariate scatterplots, with a fitted line. L. Charfeddine et al.

Research in International Business and Finance 62 (2022) 101699 increase in the spillover of shocks in the cryptocurrency market (a similar result is observed by Yi et al. (2018)) (2) the level of the own connectedness is negatively correlated with the number of cryptocurrency and the data period considered in the sample. However, when comparing the own connectedness of the mining coin block to the own connectedness of studies focusing on conventional financial market, we find evidence for strong similarity in the level of the own-effects (see for instance Diebold and Yilmaz (2014, The second important feature observed in Table 4 concerns the pairwise directional connectedness, CH i←j, that are presented by the off-diagonal elements of the upper-left 13 submatrix. The results show that the bilateral connectedness is larger between the mineable submarket coins, followed by the pairwise bilateral connectedness between mining and non-mining coins, and finally between non- mineable and token. The largest observed individual connectedness is from Ethereum to Bitcoin (CH BTC←ETH = 13.81). The second observed largest pairwise directional connectedness is from Ethereum to Bitcoin, (CH LTC←ETH = 13.02). We find also that the third and fourth largest pairwise directional connectedness involve Ethereum as transmitter, (CH EOS←ETH = 12.85) and (CH DASH←ETH = 12.22). The results also show that Ethereum is the only net transmitter to Bitcoin. However, on the other hand, we find that Augur has the lowest pairwises directional connectedness with LINK, XRP, and BTC, (CH LINK←REP = 1.17, CH XRP←REP = 1.38, and CH BTC←REP = 1.66). An inter­ esting result emerges when analyzing the pairwise connectedness between the tokens and their platform compared to their connectedness with Bitcoin. We find that for three tokens (Maker, BAT and REP) out of four cases, Ethereum has the largest values of volatility transmission. These results can be explained by the fact that all these tokens have Ethereum as a platform. The results of the two pairwise directional connectedness (the upper and lower off-diagonal connectedness table) can be used to calculate the individual connectedness known as the net pairwise directional connectedness. The results show that, in net terms, the directional connectedness take place from Ethereum to the eleven other cryptocurrencies, from Bitcoin to 10 cryptocurrencies (with Ethereum the exception), from Litecoin to 9 cryptocurrency (with Ethereum and Bitcoin as exceptions), and from EOS to 8 crypto­ currencies (except BTC, ETH and LTC). For the four tokens, they are almost net individual receivers. However, for the DASH and the three non-mining coins (XRP, XLM, and BNB), their net pairwise directional connectedness is almost negative with the mining sub­ market coin and positive with the token submarket. In nutshell, the results of the off-diagonal pairwise directional connectedness show that BTC, Ethereum, LTC and EOS are almost net pairwise directional transmitter, that all the four tokens are almost net receiver, and that non-mining coins are net transmitter to the tokens but net receiver from mining coins. The results show also that Ethereum and Augur are the net pairwise directional transmitter and the net pairwise directional receiver in the sample, respectively. 4.2.2. Total directional connectedness “from others", “to others" and the net The total directional connectedness “from others" and “to others" are defined as the sum of the row and column pairwise directional connectedness, respectively. The results of the total directional connectedness “from others" and “to others" are reported in the last column and the row TO ("to others") of Table 4, respectively. While the sum of own connectedness with the total directional connectedness “from others" is set to be equal to 100%, the sum of own connectedness with total directional connectedness “to others" is not constrained to be equal to 100%, (e.g. TO = 110.63%). The results show that mining coins are among the top five highest total connectedness “from others". In particular, we found that Ethereum has the largest value of “from others" total connectedess (CH ETH←• = 79.55%) followed by LTC and BTC. The lowest values are obtained by the four tokens with the lowest value is obtained for BAT (CH BAT←• = 18.91%). Concerning the total connectedness “to others" and similar to the results of total connectedness “from others", we found that the four mining coins are among the top five highest total connectedness “from others". Again, we found that Ethereum has the highest value (TO = 110.63%) and that ETH, BTC and LTC are ranked in the top. The results for the four tokens are also similar to the “from others" total connectedness. In particular, we find again that BAT has the lowest value (TO = 5.14%).
